item.title: holy heck +24
item.content:encoded:
per a lot of people's suggestion, took out the last addendum and added a paragraph break to make it less wall-of-text-y

but holy heck +24

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As others said, last note feels completely unnecessary to me. It's a poorly understood living creature (or behaves as one anyway) - there isn't any justification required to classify it as Euclid.
Loved the rest of it though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the thorough exploration of the effect, but the note at the end was melodramatic and I felt like sections of the article could be broken up into smaller paragraphs, especially the beginning of the description. I think in general this could probably also be summarized a little better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The final addendum is a bit overblown. Upvoted for pretty much everything else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuck's sake, I have three at home. I grew up with cats. They never managed to get near any sensitive equipment because we don't bring that stuff to my house or bring cats to the workplace, both of which are fairly commonsense measures. How the fuck do you take care of cats?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
generally aren't let near sensitive equipment

You haven't been around cats much, I take it?
Even normal cats are very, very good at getting into places they aren't supposed to be, and breaking things in the name of curiosity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminds me of a Pratchett line about how if cats acted exactly the way they really do but looked like large toads, they'd be considered horrible monsters.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is….absolutely amazing.

+1, for making me go "no fucking way, how did….holy shit" out loud at work in the morning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And I should care… why? Cats register as "nuisance" on my mental threat scale, because this scale is calibrated to real life, where cats have no anomalies to eat and generally aren't let near sensitive equipment. I'm a college student, not the guy in charge of keeping a Foundation site running.

It makes for a bad ending no matter how objectively threatening this cat would be (if it could affect anything while away from the sheet, and there's no proof of that), simply because it doesn't FEEL like anything.

Besides, if this were a serious concern, 2744 wouldn't be kept at an anomaly-containing Site anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The markings on the sheet aren't really crucial as much as mostly there for the hilarity of using sharpie to try and mimic cat characteristics (and I personally think sharpie transfer stain paw prints to be hilarious mostly for the momentary panic), so I might take some of them out.

Also, as for why Seamus is so dramatic about what is essentially an invisible cat, would you let a housecat loose in a Foundation site unattended? (he is also being kind of dramatic, but y'know)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cats could get into a lot of trouble in a Foundation site. They could eat small anomalies, and they could wander into sensitive areas and knock something over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this would probably work better if the sheet itself was given the 2744 designation, and the cat-like entity a sub-designation. From the Foundation's perspective, 2744 could reasonably be described as "a sheet that can function as though it's draped over a cat, if you let it". The cat has no verifiable existence independent of the sheet, but the sheet is a constant. Also, you describe its anomalous properties a lot, so it could stand to have a designation.

Unless there's some specific reason, I think you could stand to have the cat completely underneath an unmarked sheet. First and foremost this lets you excise a number of sentences entirely (good for an article with a concept that's basically 'bedsheet ghost cat'); second and socemost, as a cat owner, I can attest that a cat trapped completely under a bedsheet is very funny, and a cat perpetually trapped under one would be a delightful image.

The last note doesn't work for me. The only reason given for the containment procedures being anything but "fold the cloth up and put it in a plastic bag" is that maybe the cat can do mischief while not contained by the cloth, which I must emphasize is not supported by any evidence, and thus is not a hypothesis the Foundation (an ostensibly scientific organization) should be acting upon. Contrast with SCP-1422, which has the Foundation acting in-character, by my estimation.

Not to mention "it acts exactly like a cat" does nothing to strike, uh, emotions into my heart. Boy, I sure hope food containers don't start pushing themselves over table edges??? Melodramatic way to end such a cute article, if you ask me.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
"Hey ghostchibi, you haven't posted an SCP in like, years." I sure haven't.

"Hey, is this a new SCP of yours?" It sure is!

"Is it an animal again?" Yup!

"Can't you write anything other than animals?" Honestly? I don't think I could even if I tried.

henlo wiki, here is a kitty for you (and I'm having a good laugh at myself for posting this specific SCP)
